title: How parts negotiate with each other below conscious awareness
author: Kalesh

metaDescription:
The negotiation you never knew was happening 

 I was sitting in my car the other day, parked outside a grocery store, engine off. I needed to go inside....
htmlContent:
The negotiation you never knew was happening

I was sitting in my car the other day, parked outside a grocery store, engine off. I needed to go inside. I was hungry. The list was in my pocket. And I just sat there. For fifteen minutes. My hands on the wheel, my eyes on the windshield, my body completely frozen.

Here's the thing. I wasn't being lazy. I wasn't procrastinating. Something was happening below the surface, a negotiation between parts of me that I couldn't hear but could definitely feel. One part was saying, "We need food. It's safe. Let's go." Another part was saying, "Last time we went into a crowded space, we got flooded with anxiety. Remember the sweating? The racing heart? No thanks." And a third part was saying, "If we don't move, we won't have to feel any of it."

This negotiation happens constantly. Every day. Every moment. You just don't notice it because it runs beneath your conscious awareness, like an operating system you never see but that controls everything on the screen. Richard Schwartz, the founder of Internal Family Systems, calls these internal negotiations "parts interactions." He spent decades mapping how different aspects of our psyche talk to each other, sometimes collaborating, sometimes fighting, sometimes completely ignoring each other.

Right? Think about the last time you wanted to do something and couldn't. Maybe you wanted to write an email. Make a phone call. Go to the gym. And you just... didn't. That gap between intention and action? That's a negotiation. That's parts working something out below the surface.

The body is the negotiation table

Pat Ogden, a pioneer in sensorimotor psychotherapy, talks about how the body doesn't lie. Your parts don't negotiate with words. They negotiate with sensations. A tight chest. A knot in the stomach. Shallow breathing. A sudden urge to yawn. These are the voices of your parts, speaking in the only language they know.

I remember working with a client who couldn't understand why she kept sabotaging her relationships. She'd meet someone great, things would go well, and then she'd pick a fight over something small. She'd feel terrible about it afterward. She wanted to stop. But she couldn't. "It's like something takes over," she said. "I'm watching myself do it, and I can't stop."

That something was a part. A part that learned, somewhere in her history, that getting close meant getting hurt. So it negotiated with her conscious mind. It said, "I'll make you feel anxious until you push them away. Then you'll feel relief. Trust me. I've done this before." And her conscious mind, not even knowing it was in a negotiation, agreed. The body felt the anxiety. The body acted. The pattern repeated.

Stephen Porges, the researcher who developed polyvagal theory, would say this negotiation happens in the autonomic nervous system. Your parts aren't just psychological constructs. They're biological. They have neural pathways. They have physiological signatures. When a protector part steps in to keep you safe, it doesn't ask permission. It activates your sympathetic nervous system. Your heart rate changes. Your pupils dilate. Your muscles tense. The negotiation is already done before you know it started.

Stay with me here. This is where it gets wild. Because these negotiations aren't just between parts of your mind. They're between parts of your body. Your vagus nerve is sending signals to your gut. Your gut is sending signals back. Your brainstem is processing threat. Your cortex is trying to make sense of it all. And all of this is happening in milliseconds. You don't have a choice about whether these negotiations happen. You only have a choice about whether you notice them.

The silent agreements between protectors and exiles

Janina Fisher, who wrote extensively about trauma and the fragmented self, describes a dynamic that I see in almost everyone I work with. There are parts that carry the pain. She calls them exiles. And there are parts that protect you from feeling that pain. She calls them protectors. These two sets of parts have a silent agreement. The protectors say, "We'll keep the exiles hidden. We'll keep you functional. We'll keep you safe. But you have to let us run the show." And the conscious mind agrees. Not because it wants to. But because it doesn't know any other way.

A gentle approach to working with shame and self-rejection is Radical Acceptance (paid link), which helps you notice these agreements without judging them. Because the moment you judge a part for what it's doing, you create another negotiation. Now you have a part that's protecting the protector. And the whole system gets more tangled.

I see this in my own life all the time. There's a part of me that wants to be productive. It wakes up early. It makes lists. It checks things off. And there's a part of me that wants to rest. It gets tired. It scrolls social media. It avoids the lists. These two parts have been negotiating for years. The productive part says, "If you rest, you'll fail." The resting part says, "If you keep pushing, you'll burn out." And I'm stuck in the middle, feeling guilty no matter what I choose.

For understanding how trauma fragments the self, a popular choice is Healing the Fragmented Selves of Trauma Survivors (paid link). It explains how these negotiations get set up in childhood and why they persist into adulthood. The book helped me see that my parts aren't broken. They're doing exactly what they learned to do.

The negotiation between protectors and exiles is not a war. It's a cold peace. Both sides are trying to survive. Both sides think they know what's best. And both sides are partially right. The problem is that the negotiation happens so fast and so automatically that you never get a vote. You're just living the outcome.

How to interrupt the automatic negotiation

So what do you do when you realize that your life is being run by negotiations you never consented to? You start by noticing. That's it. You don't have to fix anything. You don't have to change anything. You just have to notice.

One of the foundational texts on trauma recovery is Trauma and Recovery (paid link), which outlines how awareness itself is the first step toward healing. Judith Herman, the author, talks about how trauma survivors often lose the ability to notice their own internal states. The negotiations become so fast, so habitual, that they disappear from awareness entirely.

Think about it. When was the last time you paused and asked yourself, "What part of me is running this right now?" Not what thought am I thinking. Not what emotion am I feeling. But what part of me is here? If you're like most people, the answer is never. Because we're not taught to think this way. We're taught to identify with everything that happens in our minds. We're taught to say "I am anxious" instead of "A part of me is anxious."

That shift in language is everything. When you say "a part of me," you create space. You create the possibility that another part might have a different perspective. You create room for the negotiation to become conscious. And once it's conscious, you can actually participate.

Here's a practice I use. When I notice myself stuck, frozen, or acting in a way I don't understand, I put my hand on my chest and I ask, "Who's here right now?" I wait. I don't force an answer. I just listen. Sometimes I get a word. Sometimes I get a sensation. Sometimes I get an image. But something always comes. Because the parts want to be heard. They've been negotiating in the dark for so long. They're waiting for you to turn on the light.

A gentle introduction to parts work that many people appreciate is No Bad Parts (paid link). Richard Schwartz's book walks you through how to start these conversations with your parts, how to listen without judgment, and how to let the system reorganize itself.

The negotiation never ends, and that's okay

I want to be honest with you. This doesn't get resolved. You don't wake up one day and all your parts agree. The negotiations continue. They always will. Because you're a complex being with competing needs. You want safety and adventure. You want connection and autonomy. You want to be seen and to hide. These contradictions don't go away. They just become more conscious.

What changes is your relationship to the negotiation. Instead of being a pawn in someone else's game, you become a witness. You become a mediator. You become the one who holds the space for all the parts to speak. And that changes everything.

I still sit in my car sometimes. I still freeze. I still have parts that argue with each other about whether it's safe to go into the grocery store. But now I know what's happening. I can feel the negotiation. I can say, "I hear you, protector. I know you're trying to keep me safe. And I hear you, exile. I know you're scared. We're going to go in anyway, and I'll be here the whole time."

That doesn't make the anxiety disappear. But it makes it bearable. It makes it meaningful. It turns the negotiation from a war into a conversation. And that's the whole point.

Sit with that. Notice what just happened in your body as you read these words. There's a part of you that wants to close this tab and move on. There's a part of you that wants to sit with it longer. There's a part of you that's skeptical. There's a part of you that's hopeful. They're all here. They're all negotiating. And now you know.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Trauma and Recovery (paid link) - One of the foundational texts on trauma recovery, exploring how awareness and safety rebuild the self.
  • Radical Acceptance (paid link) - A gentle approach to working with shame and self-rejection through mindfulness and compassion.
  • No Bad Parts (paid link) - A gentle introduction to parts work that many people appreciate, offering practical tools for internal dialogue.
  • Healing the Fragmented Selves of Trauma Survivors (paid link) - For understanding how trauma fragments the self, a popular choice that bridges neuroscience and therapy.
